sentences.text: It was a very calm and dreamy land.
There, dreams float by even when you're half-awake, and you can see bright castles in the clouds moving across the summer sky.
Near a place where Nicholas helps people cross, there is a small market town known as Tarry Town.
Some people call it Greensburgh, but Tarry Town is its real name.
The wives in the area say it got this name because their husbands spent too much time at the village tavern on market days.
I'm not sure if this story is true.
I'm just sharing it to give you the full picture.
Not far from Tarry Town, about two miles away, is a very quiet valley surrounded by high hills.
A small stream flows through it, making a soft sound that could make you fall asleep, and you can sometimes hear a bird or a woodpecker.
I remember going there as a young boy to try shooting a squirrel, and the loud noise of my gun in the quiet surprised me.
If I ever wanted a quiet place to escape from the world and dream, I can't think of a better place than this small valley.
This place, known as Sleepy Hollow, is quiet and its people are from the original Dutch settlers.
The land feels sleepy and dreamy, as if a spell is in the air.
Some believe a German doctor or an old Indian chief enchanted this place long ago.
It's clear that a magical power makes the people here daydream all the time.
They believe in amazing things, have visions, and often see and hear things that aren't there.
The area is full of stories about ghosts, haunted places, and superstitions; shooting stars are seen here more than anywhere else.
The main ghost of this magical place is a headless horseman.
This ghost is said to be a soldier who lost his head in a war.
He is often seen at night, moving fast as if the wind is carrying him.
He doesn't just wander in the valley but also goes to nearby roads and a church close by.
Some local experts who know a lot about this ghost say he was buried in the churchyard and goes to the battlefield at night to look for his head.
They believe he moves very fast, like the wind, because he wants to get back to the churchyard before the sun comes up.
This story has made many people in the area tell scary tales about the Headless Horseman of Sleepy Hollow.
It's interesting that not only locals but also visitors start seeing ghosts after spending some time in the valley.
Even those who didn't believe in ghosts at first begin to dream and imagine things.
I really like this quiet place because life in these Dutch valleys in New York State doesn't change, even when everything else in the country does.
These valleys are peaceful spots where life goes on slowly, not affected by the busy world outside.
Even though I haven't been to Sleepy Hollow for many years, I'm sure it's still the same, with the same trees and families living there.
About thirty years ago, a man named Ichabod Crane lived in this peaceful place.
He arrived in Sleepy Hollow to teach the kids there.
He came from Connecticut, known for its teachers and pioneers.
His name, Crane, fit him because he was very tall and skinny.
He had long arms and legs, big hands, and huge feet.
His body seemed loosely put together.
His head was small with big ears, large green eyes, and a long nose, making him look like a weather-vane.
On windy days, his clothes would flap, making him look like a scarecrow or a symbol of famine.
His school was a simple one-room log house with some glass windows and others patched with workbook pages.
The school was locked with a twisted branch and stakes, making it easy to get in but hard to get out, similar to an eel trap.
It was located in a quiet, nice place by a brook and a big birch tree at the bottom of a hill.
On a quiet summer day, you could hear Ichabod's students studying, sometimes interrupted by his strict voice or the sound of discipline.
Ichabod believed in strict discipline, thinking it was necessary for the children's growth.
But Ichabod was fair, not just strict.
He was kinder to the weaker students and tougher on the stronger ones.
He treated sensitive kids gently and was stricter with the stubborn ones.
Ichabod thought he was helping the children by teaching them hard lessons.
After school, he liked to spend time with the older students and walk the younger ones home, especially if they had nice sisters or mothers who cooked well.
He wanted to keep a good relationship with his students.
Ichabod didn't earn much from teaching, just enough to buy food, even though he was thin but ate a lot.
To make extra money, he lived with his students' families, moving from one house to another every week with his things in a handkerchief.
To help his rural customers who found education expensive and thought schoolmasters were lazy, he found ways to be useful and likable.
He helped farmers with simple tasks like making hay, fixing fences, taking horses to water, driving cows, and chopping wood.
He became very gentle and friendly, different from how he was at school.
He made the mothers like him by taking good care of the children and even rocking cradles with his foot.
He also taught singing to the young people in the area and earned money from it.
On Sundays, he proudly led the choir in church and felt he was better than the preacher.
His voice was so strong that it could be heard across the millpond on quiet Sundays.
By doing many small things, he managed to live well and seemed to have an easy life to those who didn't understand the hard work of teaching.
In rural areas, a schoolmaster is often seen as important and more sophisticated than local men, except for the preacher.
So, his visits to farmhouses often meant extra treats and the use of fancy teapots.
The local young women really liked our scholar.
He was very popular, especially when he picked grapes for them, read tombstone writings, or walked with them near the millpond, while the shy local men felt jealous.
He traveled a lot and shared news and gossip from house to house, making everyone happy to see him.
The women thought he was very smart because he read many books and knew a lot about witchcraft from Cotton Mather's book, which he believed in completely.
He was a mix of being a little smart and very gullible.
His love for unbelievable stories grew stronger in this magical place.
He believed every strange tale.
After teaching, he liked to lie on the clover near his school and read scary stories until it got dark.
As he walked home through the spooky woods and swamps, every sound made his imagination run wild.
The calls of the whip-poor-will, the croak of the toad, the owl's hoot, and the rustle of birds made him think of magical things.
Sometimes, a very bright firefly would surprise him as it flew by.
If a big beetle bumped into him, he would get scared, thinking it was a witch's spell.
Singing hymns was his way to feel better or keep away bad spirits.
People in Sleepy Hollow loved listening to his singing from far away or down a quiet road.
He also liked to spend winter evenings with old Dutch women, listening to their stories by the fire.
They told him about ghosts, haunted places, and the headless horseman.
He shared his own stories about witchcraft and strange signs in the sky from Connecticut's past.
He would frighten them with talk about comets, shooting stars, and how the world spins.
These cozy times by the fire were nice, but the fear on his walk home was a big downside.
Scary shapes and shadows would appear on his way home in the weak light of a snowy night.
He always looked for a light from a far window across the fields.
Often, he was scared by a bush covered in snow because it looked like a ghost.
He shook with fear when he heard his own steps on the cold ground, scared to look back in case something was following him.
Many times, he thought a sudden wind was the Galloping Hessian coming for him.
But all these fears were just his imagination at night.
Even though he saw many ghosts and faced Satan on his walks, the daylight made these fears go away.
He could have been happy despite the Devil, if not for one thing more troubling than ghosts—a woman.
Katrina Van Tassel, the daughter of a rich Dutch farmer, was one of his music students.
She was a lively 18-year-old, healthy and beautiful, with cheeks like her father’s peaches.
She was known not just for her looks, but also for her wealth.
She liked to flirt and showed it in her clothes, mixing old and new styles to look her best.
She wore gold jewelry from Saardam, an old-fashioned bodice, and a short skirt to show off her pretty foot and ankle.
Ichabod Crane liked women a lot, so he quickly found this young woman very attractive, especially after visiting her family's home.
Old Baltus Van Tassel was a successful and happy farmer who was very generous.
He didn't care much about things outside his farm, but everything inside was cozy and well-organized.
He was proud of his wealth but not in a show-off way; he cared more about being generous than living in style.
His home was by the Hudson River, in a green and fertile spot loved by Dutch farmers.
A big elm tree stood over it, with a spring underneath that flowed into a nearby brook.
Next to the farmhouse was a huge barn, filled with the farm's products and always busy with work.
Inside, you could hear threshing all day, and outside, birds flew and rested in the sun.
Fat pigs lay in their pens, sometimes joined by little piglets who came out to smell the air.
White geese swam in a pond, leading ducks, while turkeys and Guinea fowls walked around the farm, making noise.
A rooster walked proudly in front of the barn, showing off and calling his family to eat when he found food.
Ichabod felt very hungry looking at all the animals, thinking of them as delicious food.
He imagined the pigs with apples in their mouths and the birds ready to be cooked in different ways.
He saw the animals as future meals, like bacon, ham, and even the rooster ready to be eaten.
As he dreamed about the food, he also thought about the rich lands and fruits at the Van Tassel's home.
He imagined turning everything into money to buy land and build homes.
He pictured a future with Katrina and their children, moving to a new place with all their belongings.
When he entered the house, he was completely in love.
It was a big farmhouse with a high, sloping roof, built like the houses of the first Dutch settlers.
The roof came down low, making a porch that could be closed when the weather was bad.
Under this porch, there were tools for farming and fishing nets for the river nearby.
There were benches for sitting in summer, a big spinning wheel, and a churn, showing the porch was used for many things.
Ichabod walked from this porch into the main living area of the house, amazed.
Inside, he saw shiny dishes on a dresser that caught his eye.
There was a big bag of wool ready to be spun, homemade cloth, and decorations of corn, dried apples, peaches, and red peppers on the walls.
He also saw a fancy room with shiny chairs and tables, fireplace tools hidden among plants, and decorations of shells and bird eggs.
From the moment Ichabod saw all this, he couldn't stop thinking about winning the heart of Van Tassel's daughter.
But he knew winning her love would be harder than the fairy tale challenges of knights, like fighting dragons, because those knights always won easily and got to marry the lady.
Ichabod had to win the heart of a girl who liked to play games, and he also had to beat many local men who liked her too.
The toughest of these men was Abraham, also called Brom Van Brunt, a strong and brave local hero.
Brom was big, had short black hair, and a face that looked rough but kind, showing he liked fun and was a bit proud.
Everyone called him BROM BONES because he was very strong.
He was the best at riding horses and winning fights.
His strength made him the leader in the area, and he solved problems with confidence that no one argued with.
Brom liked to have fun and fight, but he was never really mean and had a good sense of humor.
He had friends who looked up to him, and they all liked to find adventures or trouble together.
In winter, Brom wore a fur cap with a fox's tail, and seeing this at parties meant trouble was coming.
Sometimes, people heard him and his friends making noise at night and knew it was Brom Bones and his gang causing trouble, but they still liked and respected him.
A wild hero liked Katrina very much and showed it in a clumsy way, but she didn't seem to mind.
His actions scared off other men who liked her, making them not want to compete.
When his horse was seen at Katrina's home one Sunday night, it meant he was there to see her, making other men lose hope.
Ichabod Crane had a very tough competitor, and most men would have given up or not even tried.
But Ichabod was different; he knew how to adapt and keep trying without giving up.
He was strong in a way that he could handle pressure and still stand tall after it was gone.
Fighting directly with his rival would have been foolish because this rival was very hard to beat in matters of love.
So, Ichabod chose a clever and quiet way to be near Katrina.
He acted as if he was just there to teach singing.
This way, he could visit her house often without causing suspicion.
He wasn't worried about the parents getting in the way, which can be a big problem for people in love.
Balt Van Tassel was easy-going and loved his daughter more than anything, letting her do what she wanted.
His wife was always busy with the house and her chickens, thinking they needed more care than a girl.
So, while she was busy, Balt would relax with his pipe, watching a toy soldier fight the wind on the barn roof.
Meanwhile, Ichabod tried to win the daughter's heart by the spring or during evening walks, which was a perfect time for love.
I don't really understand how to win a woman's heart.
To me, it's always been something hard to figure out.
Some women seem to have just one way to win their heart, while others have many ways, and winning them over can be done in so many different ways.
It's a big deal to win the heart of the first type, but it's even harder to keep the second type interested, as a man must always try to keep her attention.
Winning over many is impressive, but keeping a flirt interested is truly a great feat.
Brom Bones was not happy when Ichabod started to win over Katrina.
Brom's horse was no longer seen outside on Sunday nights, and he became rivals with Ichabod, the teacher of Sleepy Hollow.
Brom wanted to fight Ichabod like the old knights did.
But Ichabod avoided fighting because he knew Brom was stronger.
Ichabod had heard Brom say he would beat him up, so he stayed away.
Brom got frustrated and decided to play tricks on Ichabod instead.
Brom and his friends made fun of Ichabod, messed up his singing classes, and made the school look haunted at night.
Brom even joked about Ichabod in front of Katrina and made his dog imitate Ichabod's singing.
Brom's pranks on Ichabod kept going, but nothing really changed between them.
They kept competing for Katrina without a clear winner.
One sunny autumn afternoon, Ichabod was sitting on his tall stool, thinking deeply in his small school.
He had a ruler in his hand, showing he was in charge, and a birch rod for punishment was hanging behind him.
On his desk, there were things he took from his students, like half-eaten apples and toys.
It looked like he had just punished someone because all the students were either studying or talking quietly, watching Ichabod.
Suddenly, a Black man in simple clothes and a hat came on a young horse, holding a rope as a bridle.
He came to invite Ichabod to a party at Mynheer Van Tassel’s house that evening.
After giving his message, he quickly left, showing how important his task was.
The classroom became noisy and busy right away.
The students hurried with their work, some skipping parts, and Ichabod helped or motivated them to finish.
Then, the school ended an hour early, and the students ran out, playing and shouting, happy to be free.
Ichabod spent an extra thirty minutes getting ready, making his old black suit look nice and fixing his hair with a broken mirror.
He wanted to impress his lady and borrowed a horse from Hans Van Ripper, the farmer he lived with.
Riding this horse, Ichabod felt like a knight going on an adventure.
Now, let me tell you about Ichabod and his horse.
The horse, Gunpowder, was old and known for being grumpy.
It was skinny, with a weird neck and a head like a hammer; its hair was messy and tangled.
One of its eyes was blind and scary, but the other eye was playful.
Despite looking worn out, Gunpowder was still full of energy, probably because Van Ripper, who liked to ride fast, had owned it.
This horse, even looking old, seemed more lively than younger horses.
Ichabod was the perfect person to ride Gunpowder.
He rode with his knees high and elbows out, looking like a grasshopper.
He held his whip up high, and his arms moved like he had wings.
A small wool hat was on his head, and his coat was very long.
Ichabod and his horse, Gunpowder, looked very funny as they left Hans Van Ripper's farm.
It was a beautiful autumn day with a clear sky.
The trees were brown and yellow, but some were bright orange, purple, and red.
High in the sky, there were groups of wild ducks flying.
You could hear squirrels and the sound of quails from the fields.
The small birds were having a great time eating.
They jumped and played around, happy with all the food they found.
Cock robin was an honest bird, loved by young hunters for its loud cries.
Blackbirds flew around in big groups, and the woodpecker showed off its red head and beautiful feathers.
The cedar bird had red on its wings and yellow on its tail, wearing a small feathered hat.
The blue jay, a noisy bird, wore a bright blue and white coat, always making a lot of noise.
As Ichabod rode, he was happy to see so much food in the autumn.
He saw many apples, some on trees, some in baskets, and others ready for making cider.
He also saw fields of corn and pumpkins, promising good food like cakes and pies.
Passing buckwheat fields, he dreamed of eating pancakes made by Katrina Van Tassel.
His journey took him by beautiful views of the Hudson River and the Tappan Zee.
The setting sun and calm sky made the river and cliffs look even more beautiful.
A boat was floating far away, moving slowly with the water, its sail hanging down; it looked like it was in the air because of the sky's reflection on the water.
Ichabod arrived at the Van Tassel's big house in the evening, where important people from the area were gathered.
There were old farmers dressed in simple clothes, with big shoes and shiny buckles.
Their wives wore tight caps, short dresses, and had scissors and colorful pockets showing.
The young women looked a bit more modern with straw hats, bright ribbons, or white dresses.
The sons had jackets with big buttons and sometimes tied their hair back, using eel-skin because it was thought to make hair stronger.
But everyone was looking at Brom Bones, who came on his horse Daredevil, a horse that only he could manage.
Brom liked wild animals that could do tricks and thought calm horses were boring for young men.
I wish I could tell you more about the amazing things Ichabod saw inside the Van Tassel's house.
Especially the beautiful women, their dresses, and the amazing Dutch tea-table filled with food during the autumn.
There were so many cakes made by skilled Dutch housewives.
They had doughnuts, oly koeks, crullers, and many other kinds of cakes.
There were also apple, peach, and pumpkin pies, along with ham, smoked beef, and preserved fruits like plums and peaches.
They had broiled shad, roasted chickens, milk, cream, and a warm teapot in the middle.
I wish I had more time to talk about this feast, but I want to continue my story.
Ichabod Crane enjoyed the food very much and felt very happy.
He dreamed of owning all this wealth and leaving his old life behind.
Baltus Van Tassel made everyone feel welcome with his happy and friendly ways.
He greeted his guests warmly, telling them to help themselves.
Then, music from the hall invited everyone to dance.
The musician was an old man with gray hair who had been playing music alone in the area for over fifty years.
His instrument looked as old as he did.
He usually played on just two or three strings and moved a lot when he played, even stamping his foot when people started to dance.
Ichabod was very proud of how well he could dance and sing.
He moved so much that you would think he was the patron saint of dance himself.
He made all the black people who came to watch, young and old, very happy.
They stood at the doors and windows, smiling and enjoying the show.
Ichabod was very happy, especially because he was dancing with the woman he liked, even though Brom Bones was jealous and sat alone, not happy at all.
After the dance, Ichabod joined some older people, including Old Van Tassel, who were talking and remembering old times.
They were on the porch, smoking and sharing stories about the past and the war.
This neighborhood was special because it had a lot of history and famous people.
During the war, it was close to where British and American soldiers fought.
Many people, including soldiers and refugees, came here.
Over time, people started to make their stories more exciting.
They added things that didn't happen, making themselves the heroes.
There was a big Dutchman named Doffue Martling who had a blue beard.
He tried to take over a British ship with an old cannon.
But his cannon broke after he fired it six times.
There was also a very important man who is too famous to mention quickly.
In the battle of White Plains, he was so good at fighting that he stopped a bullet with his sword.
He said he could feel the sword buzz and hit the handle.
He showed his bent sword handle as proof.
Many others were also very brave in the fight.
Each believed they had a big part in winning the war.
But their stories were not as interesting as the ghost stories.
The area is full of famous ghost stories.
In these old, quiet places, local stories and beliefs are common.
But in busy towns, these stories are often forgotten.
Ghosts don't have much of a chance in our villages.
They hardly have time to become ghosts before their friends leave.
At night, they have no friends to visit.
This may be why we only hear about ghosts in old Dutch places.
The main reason for these ghost stories is Sleepy Hollow's location.
The air from Sleepy Hollow brings strange feelings.
It makes everyone dream and imagine things.
Many people from Sleepy Hollow were at Van Tassel's house.
They were telling their amazing stories as they always do.
They talked about seeing ghosts at funerals and hearing cries near the tree where Major André was caught.
They also spoke about a woman in white who haunts Raven Rock.
She screams before a storm, because she died there in the snow.
Many stories are told about Sleepy Hollow's ghost, the Headless Horseman.
People say he has been seen in the area recently.
They believe he ties his horse in the churchyard among the graves every night.
This church is a known place for ghosts because it is hidden away.
It sits on a hill, surrounded by trees.
The church's white walls look simple and pure in the dark.
There is a slope that goes down to a bright lake, surrounded by big trees.
Through the trees, you can see the blue hills of the Hudson.
The sunny graveyard seems like a peaceful place for the dead to rest.
Near the church, there is a big valley with a stream running through it.
Near the church, there was a wooden bridge over a dark stream.
The road and bridge were hidden by thick trees, making it very dark even in the daytime and very scary at night.
This place was where the Headless Horseman liked to be and was often seen.
There is a story about a man named old Brouwer, who did not believe in ghosts.
One night, coming back from Sleepy Hollow, he met the Horseman and had to ride with him.
They went fast through the woods until they got to the bridge.
At the bridge, the Horseman changed into a skeleton, threw Brouwer into the water, and vanished with a loud noise.
After this, Brom Bones told an even more amazing story.
Brom, who did not believe in the Galloping Hessian, said that one night, after leaving Sing Sing, the Horseman caught up with him.
Brom raced him for a bowl of punch and was about to win because of his fast horse, Daredevil.
As they got close to the church bridge, the Hessian vanished with a flash of fire.
Ichabod was very scared by these stories told in the dark.
He told his own stories from Cotton Mather and spoke about weird happenings in Connecticut and frightening things in Sleepy Hollow.
The party came to an end.
The farmers took their families home in wagons, making noise on the roads and hills.
Some girls rode with their boyfriends, laughing until it all went quiet.
Ichabod stayed to talk with the rich girl, hoping she liked him.
No one knows what they talked about.
But something went wrong because he left looking sad.
Maybe the girl was just pretending to like him to make another man jealous.
Only Heaven knows what happened.
Ichabod left feeling guilty, like he did something wrong, not like he won a lady's heart.
He didn't enjoy the views of the countryside that he usually loved.
He went straight to the stable, woke up his horse, and got ready to leave.
The horse, which was dreaming of lots of food, was suddenly woken up.
It was very late at night when Ichabod, feeling sad, made his way home.
He traveled along the high hills above Tarry Town, which he had been happy to cross earlier.
He felt as gloomy as the hour was.
Far below, the Tappan Zee looked dark and unclear.
He could see the tall masts of boats and hear a dog barking from the other side of the Hudson.
He felt very far from his dog, which usually stayed close to him.
Sometimes, he could hear a rooster crowing from far away, coming from a farmhouse in the hills.
This sound seemed like it was part of a dream.
Around him, there were only small signs of life, like a cricket's sad chirp or the rough sound of a bullfrog from a nearby marsh.
He remembered all the scary stories about ghosts and goblins he had heard earlier.
The night became darker, making the stars look further away and sometimes clouds would cover them.
He had never felt so alone and scared.
He was getting closer to the place where many scary stories were set.
There was a huge tulip-tree in the middle of the road, taller than the other trees and used as a landmark.
The branches of the tree were big and twisted, reaching down to the ground and then up again.
This tree was linked to the sad story of André, who was caught near it and was known as Major André's tree.
People felt a mix of respect and fear for it, feeling sorry for André and scared by stories of weird sights and sounds.
When Ichabod came near this scary tree, he started to whistle.
He thought someone whistled back, but it was just the wind in the dry branches.
Getting closer, he saw something white in the tree and stopped whistling to look.
He realized it was just a part of the tree hit by lightning, showing the white wood.
Then, he heard a groan, his teeth chattered, and his knees shook.
But it was only the noise of a big branch moving in the wind.
He went past the tree without harm, but more danger was ahead.
A small brook crossed the road near the tree, leading into a swampy, wooded area called Wiley's Swamp.
Some rough logs placed together made a bridge over the stream.
Near the road where the stream went into the woods, there were oaks and chestnuts covered in wild grapevines making a dark shadow.
Crossing this bridge was very hard.
This is where André was caught because people hid under the trees and vines and surprised him.
Since then, people think this place is haunted, and schoolboys are scared to pass it alone at night.
